item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: As the author of the article, I agree with what ARD said. In this case, the proposed reference seems kind of random, and not actually connected to what the article intends. I'd rather not have this change be made.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: The main reason why I chose SCP-1541 is because the name of the god itself is redacted in the skip, so linking it to this one, where the name IS specified, would result in as little headcanon-crushing as possible. Using something from the Second Hytoth gives me some pause, for that reason.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: So far none of the Ortothan mythos gods are skips, buuuuut if an alien SCP that believes in the mythos (which could easily have Gizyxs as one of the many gods in it) then SCP-3417 is the best candidate for this.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I don't like the proposed crosslink because of how arbitrary it feels - the choice of god doesn't have any real backing behind "it exists so use it". While I don't have any good alternatives in mind, I would take a look through the articles tagged second-hytoth to see if any of those would fit, since the Church of the Second Hytoth's whole shtick is that it's an extraterrestrial religion brought to Earth.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author vetoed edit

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

I feel like this could be linked to SCP-1541. This mentions some kind of deity named "Gizyxs". I feel like this deity could potentially be SCP-1541, since SCP-1541's name isn't actually mentioned in the skip itself.

Editing this:

SCP-2775: Gizyxs damn it.

To this:

SCP-2775: Gizyxs damn it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels too much like 1532 and 2699 for me to like it. The article itself also feels bare, and nothing stood out to me.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2775: Yeah, uh, it was some place called China.

Is that the punchline? It killed me absolutely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There really is no reason for the alien to be in this article. This would have worked just as well with a human customer as the caller.

Also, as some have pointed out, this is more of an anomalous event than a skip. If the Human Mills Company was the focus, and not the call itself, then I could see this working as a skip, but as it stands the most intriguing part of the article is relegated to the background in favor of humor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, real question… why did the Foundation do that? Why did they intercept that second call and convince the alien that it bought bootleg humans? They're trying to contain this thing! They want to get as much information as possible and keep it on the line, not convince it to hang up and never call back!

It makes no narrative sense whatsoever, and I suspect the author just wanted to continue his tired, hacky call center joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another alien that actually manages to be funnier than SCP-2020.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spot the difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's just a tech support joke.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although I do like the concept, I wasn't surprised at all when the person buying the humans turned out to be an alien. I found that a bit too expected. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is interesting, but the second interview doesn't really sound like it's coming from the Foundation. The whole thing about Chinese bootleg humans makes me think that that should be coming from the Human Mills company itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, thanks for clearing that up. One less problem with this skip, but the tone of the entity is still too glaring of an issue to allow me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought weizhong said that phone calls could be tracked and pinpointed.

It was. From beyond the Solar System. In this scenario, the company might just be the distribution/production center, and only takes orders from the Customer Service guys. To track them to the Southwest probably means they're not using a return address, but they ate using a postal service UPS, FedEx, USPS, etc.) to ship out these humans. That means the Foundation can track down to the initial offices these things shipped from. And if Human Mills Company has multiple warehouses in the Southwest… That will make them harder to track, as they are all unlikely to all use the same postal office to ship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another disappointment.
At first , I wanted to upvote because "Human Mills" is the sort of a name and linked concept that's inevitably leading to something cool.

Except it didn't - the way this ended up going is another of those HUMANS GO HOME style articles, which is honestly getting far more tiring than talking animals ever did. Thekillerax got it right with referring to Kalinin's criticism, except, you know - in the case of the succubus I actually liked the way she talked, and it made some sense if you turn your head sideways and squint a little.
Here… it doesn't.

Ultimately this is the sort of thing, down with them talking exactly like tech support, I'd have expected from a Mitchell and Webb sketch rather than something meant to be a plausible mainlister, and even then it wouldn't rank among my liked sketches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Bootleg Chinese humans" is hilarious. The rest, not so much.

For one thing, my immediate thought was "don't we already have an extraterrestrial phone signal skip?" For another, this does that thing that irks me with non-Earth language interviews, in that it gives the exact spelling of an unknown word. It looks like you'd pronouce "Gizyxs" "GIZZ-iks-iz". But maybe not. How do we know it's got an i and a y? Why one z and not two or three? If the s is silent, why is it there? It doesn't make any sense to have that word in the interview, not with that specific a spelling, anyway. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, we already know that it's in the southwestern US, which makes scouring for it significantly easier. I mean, how did we even find out that's it located there? Do we have tech that's able to pinpoint the region but not the exact location? I thought weizhong said that phone calls could be tracked and pinpointed. Is there anomalous hiding techniques at play here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think we don't understand them or where do they come from. If the Foundation does not know who made it and how does it work, they simply don't bother using it, specially if they know using them could mess their test results.

Besides, we already have cloning skips. Not all that special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the bootleg hoomans, or the humans from human mill, would be a viable alternative to D class personnell. what do you guys think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Second one is probably just me being dense, but how has the Foundation not located this company yet?

I think that even with the most powerful headcanon version of the Foundation, it will still take them a while to find a needle in a haystack, even assuming that all of their information is correct. Remember: the Foundation ultimately relies on the human element to get shit done, and all humans are fallible.

Also, you're assuming that the Human Mills Company is legitimate and is currently operating with all the required licenses. For all we know, it could illegally be operating out of some shmuck's garage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
fairenough.exe

^It's a phone call tracing program.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more I read this, the less funny it becomes.
The first time around, I found myself positively rolling with laughter. Bootleg humans being treated like defective Apple products is a hilarious concept, and the "reboot" won me over.
On the second read, however, it had lost some of its original charm because I had read it previously, and the flaws started to make themselves apparent.
Biggest issue right off the bat is something Kalinin has referred to as "unknowable eldritch horror which talks like Dave from Accounting." This entity lives in an entirely different dimension to us, and that has undoubtedly shaped their lingo and conversational lexicon. To have this thing speak like you or I when we phone in to Apple to complain about our products makes this more relatable and therefore more humorous, but ultimately detracts from the article for me. Yes, you did go with the whole "replace 'God' in 'goddammit' with some other name" to make him sound more alien, but honestly, I feel that that trope has been exhausted to the point where seeing it just makes me cringe a bit (bonus points for making the deity's name have a bunch of Z's and X's.)
I'm not implying that you have to change anything, as it seems that most people are fine with it, but I'm just saying that that particular issue irks me a lot. I know that the actual entity isn't the focus of the article; the company and its products are, but it still annoyed me quite a lot.
Second one is probably just me being dense, but how has the Foundation not located this company yet? If we know that the company is located in the southwestern United States, couldn't we just comb through each suspicious company till we found it? I mean, I know it's a bit meticulous, but this is the Foundation we're talking about, and considering this is a Euclid-class object, I'd assume that they'd go to the trouble of doing it anyway. How did we even manage to trace them to the southwestern US in the first place?
Anyway, I still find this pretty amusing, despite the personal quibbles I had with it, so it balances out to a novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you able to access your human's menu?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's part of the "WTF" factor of it. In reality, you can't, but in this interpretation, you can. Part of the hand-waving aspect.

Since in the real world, we've never had to deal with anything like this, there's no way to do it, but in this world, I'm just going to say that we can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, but… okay, admittedly, I'm about as knowledgeable in physics as you'd expect from many lowly bio-scientists (medic, at that… "c" is shorthand for "see" to me), but the problem lays with the FTL part. How do we track FTL radiation? How do we know the comms aren't linking the phone grid with a satellite in deep space, not outside the Sol system but far away enough from Earth as to track it?

Again, scientific ignorance here, will appreciate clarification. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Phone calls can be traced by their origin like any other signal sent from anywhere. The signal has to have an origin point, and that's easily traced.

As for the technological part, I mentioned this to a few members in chat before I posted, but I felt like hand-waving that aspect as I felt it would make it unnecessarily muddled. I'll add it in now though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Then, why does the document give such relevance to this:

SCP-2775 is capable of speaking in English despite its origin. The anomaly is noted to have an accent of indeterminate origin. It is unknown how SCP-2775 acquired its knowledge of English.

and doesn't even mention the amazing technological and scientific achievement of FTL communications and its implications?

I mean, I'm totally okay with the article and upvoted, but it is sort of jarring, isn't it? :O

Edit: furthermore, how do we even track a FTL phone calls made from beyond the limits of the Solar System? :I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmmmmmmmmmm…. ok. I get that, and to be fair, I've done something similar before. Ok, downvote rescinded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops. That's my bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, why does the alien says "God dammit"? Shouldn't it say "Gizyxs dammit"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The anomaly is located somewhere outside of our solar system, knows a disturbing amount about Earthly culture/norms, and it can make a phone call faster than light (since in order to call like this with no latency, the phone signal must be able to travel faster than light to reach that point and back) which implies highly advanced technology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like, it's well written and the dialogue is funny, but this really doesn't seem that anomalous to me. The only evidence they've got of its existence is some phone calls, and some uncertain locations where those calls originated from. I don't see exactly what it does, and for that reason I have to down vote.

That being said, if somebody can adequately explain that I'm missing something, i can amend my vote. Funny phone calls are funny, but not exactly a foundation caliber concern.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better than I imagined. It is beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is basically just the same conversation I had over the phone about the shitty mp3 player I ordered except with humans being the item in question. Yet I upvoted without hesitation. Why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure why I upvoted. The comedy isn't hitting me very hard, and neither is the horror. It reminds me of SCP-2222 in its cold treatment of human beings, and of the Unspeakable Vault of Doom in its absurdity. However, this doesn't have nearly as much meat on its bones as the two things I linked. Still, I'm leaving the upvote because it could just be that I'm tired.
Edit: Now that I've read the forum post that lead to the creation of this, I'm far more amused. My +1 is now enthusiastic.

Edit 2: Changing to downvote. This is more like an Extranormal event than an SCP. Oh, some alien made a phone call that breaks logic and causality? Big fuckin deal, 682 just breached containment and killed a dozen employees, the DEER has decided we're not giving it enough sacrifices, and the deal with the CotBG involving 2217 just went sour, plus some dumb bastard let 447-2 touch a dead body. This wouldn't even register on the Foundation's radar unless Human Mills were located and it turns out they're mass-producing philosophical zombies and selling them in bulk to extraterrestrials. THAT would warrant SCP classification while still allowing room for the original idea to hang out in the article

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
This was written as a response to Bright's Challenge Thread, specifically this post in particular. Many thanks to all of the members of chat who helped me flesh this out, though special thanks go out to TwistedGears for the wonderful line about taking shoes off and on, as well as CryogenChaos for helping me to make this less reminiscent of a certain SMBC strip, and of course, GobTraz for proposing the idea in the first place.

There's not much else to say about this one. It was short, sweet, and fun to write. Hope you all enjoy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All of my yes.
